author: @gen81465
text:
Here's an interesting rocket stove idea, using bricks, that also doesn't require using any tools to assemble: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=onMbSLuC7Oc
.
Today's prices for bricks (at my local Lowe's store) would be 91¢ for a standard concrete masonry brick, and $4.68 for a "fire brick" (aka a "refractory brick") that's designed for high heat applications. As the presenter on the video pointed out, either brick will do: it's just a matter of how much you're willing to spend (25 bricks needed, so price range $25 USD to $125 USD). 
.
Several things to keep in mind, however, if you go with the brick method:
1. Less expensive, but also less portable. Of course, I won't be slipping either version into my backpack real soon (for that I use a Kelly Kettle), but with a ring type handle attached, I believe the pipe version could be quite easily carried. Not much you can do with carrying bricks except drop one on your foot, claim to be injured, and wait for your friend to carry them.
2. Bricks are much less weather tolerant, especially the concrete bricks, as they absorb rainwater quite handily. I've heard horror stories of people going camping and lining the perimeter of their fire pit with stones from a local creek or river. As the stones heat up, the water that soaks into them (and it will) eventually sizzles out and they can crack and split as they dry out. Now imagine spilling water on them from something you're cooking, and enjoy a lovely display from a "Mother Nature's hand grenade".

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Suggestion: for a handle for the lid, if you want to avoid drilling holes or relying on a wrench, perhaps one can find just the right sized constrictor band from a coffee urn handle.

I admit, I do not know if one does exist, but I do know a lot of different sizes do exist, and most of them can easily be removed with a single screw driver, and you can slip some other kind of handle on it, should the handle it comes with, tend toward hanging up on the body of the stove.

In such a case, also, one could consider sawing or grinding the handle short enough to clear the stove body...

The reason I say 'or grind' is because one merely needs some weathered concrete as a grinding medium, therefore, leaving the one and only tool to be the screwdriver.

Just put the parts that hang down too far against the concrete or other readily available abrasive surface, and drag it until enough of it wears off that the remainder will spin past the body of the stove and you are in business.

I suppose, if you are really skilled, and not too worried about getting a jagged, uneven break, some handles could just be wedged between any 2 strong, immobile points [I have seen porch railings made out of 'wrought iron' where there was a pretty decently spaced gap that could serve for this, as well as certain types of lugs on the beds of [how you say in the UK 'lorries'?] flat bed trucks which can serve as solid places to snap off the excess.

author: @samuelfellows6923
text: It could be turned into a masonry stove - the back of the stove is set into a brick base with the chimney inside ~ a gas bottle cut in half and placed over it and on top of the base/a steel oil barrel - ether painted stove black, a brick column where the the heat will flow up the internal chimney and down the side of it and out a hole in the back of the base; to a outer chimney/a masonry brick bench with a hollow passage, with dividers for the heat to flow around, and the chimney being put at the end of the beach/a heat-proof centrifugal fan to start the fire/chimney draw. If it is against a external wall - the air intake could have an elbow facing the wall and a pipe put through it to a vent outside ~ using outside air for combustion = keeping the heat inside the room with no drafts. If the base of the oil drum/gas bottle is flat enough ~ will function as a hob for kettle/frying-saucepans, and if seasoned with oil = a hot-plate for frying.

author: @paulwheaton
text: With a rocket mass heater, we are shooting for temperatures closing in one 2000 degrees F.  Note that steel spalls at 1600 degrees F.  So a steel j-tube tends to fail.  Of course with a 4-inch system, the amount of wood is small enough you often cannot get to 1600 degrees F.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not shooting for that - I did a video on this topic - rocket stoves explained

replies.author: @paulwheaton
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Watched it.  What you have is a j-tube, which isn't usually a rocket stove, but I do think a j-tube rocket is far better than an L-tube rocket.  Good!  And, yes, you are not shooting for a rocket mass heater - although it is rocket mass heaters that typically use the j-tube.  Although the dimensions you mention are the rmh j-tube dimensions (sort of).

author: @jerrodlopes186

Awesome.   I would personally use a "Y" rather than  a "T" for where you stick the wood.
And standard single wall stove pipe in 6" available at any big box should have everything one needs to do that doubled chimnea thing just off the shelf.  Might be a touch tricky to seal it at the bottom but a wall collar, some of that sealant, and maybe some tin snips should do it.  I even think I recall a 6 to 4" adapter that you could wrangle for your exhaust, but one could be cobbled easy enough.

Also, on your handle (as i have that issue with my pots at home) - I find a large 2 prong fork like you use for carving turkey does the trick well.

author: @THEGLASSMANSWORLD
text:
I really love your videos and your enthusiasm!  This build is brilliant!
I started making Rocket Stoves years ago and the ratio I remember is, if the air opening or fire feed hole is 4" diameter, then the pipe has to be 4" all the way through, and the out pipe must be 3xs as tall as the opening, so at least 12" tall for a 4" opening.

replies.author: @THEGLASSMANSWORLD
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I also believe if the top is tapered inward it may create a more powerful outflame which could mimick how the made glass beads in Egypt over 2000 years ago. I blow glass and have always wanted to attempt this build! Maybe soon unless you beat me to it.

author: @dougpine4746
text: As a side note a well tuned rocket burns complete enough to be CO2 negative compared to the same amount of wood sitting on the ground rotting. Its been researched and tested to death. I limit my chimney to 115 degrees f. Trying a different wind proof cap this winter so I can drop my chimney temp to 75 -80 f . The heat belongs in the house not outside. Less wood less work.

replies.author: @johndough8115
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Small Pipe Fittings, cost a fortune.  Finding pipe fittings as large as yours, is neither not easy.. and typically, is not Cheap.  (And if you got them used.. good luck on the threads not being damaged)

 I actually bought some 4" diameter Steel Tube for a special project.  Three 10ft pipes, cost me a bit over $300 (sch 40 thickness).

 Meanwhile, anyone can grab a box of bricks for cheap... or even, are often found Free.

 There are vids that show these brick versions being assembled, quickly and easily.   One version, uses Cinder-Blocks.
None have to be sealed together permanently, if you just want to experiment.

 That said, unless its high temp bricks, they will likely, eventually crack.  Still will probably function though.

text: Great design, and it should appeal to those who like "industrial" design.  Note that Diesel Truck exhaust pipe can be had online in 2 and 4 ft lengths, for near the avg cost of your other components.  There are some nice exhaust pipe joining "clamps" for extending the length, too.
